name: Herpes Zoster (Shingles)
ontario_ma_scope: Herpes zoster IS in Ontario MA scope (added Oct 2023). Eligible: immunocompetent adult, rash <72h OR new vesicles still forming, non-ophthalmic, non-otic, non-disseminated, non-pregnant.

key_questions: Classic prodrome: dermatomal pain/burning/tingling for 2–3 days before rash?
Rash: unilateral, dermatomal vesicular rash (does NOT cross midline)?
Age ≥50 or immunocompromised (highest risk for complications)?
Ophthalmic (forehead/eye) or otic (ear) distribution → urgent referral?
Time from rash onset: antivirals most effective within 72h
red_flags: Ophthalmic zoster (V1 — forehead/nose/eye rash) → same-day ophthalmology — corneal scarring risk
Ramsay Hunt syndrome: ear pain + vesicles in ear canal + facial palsy → urgent ENT/neurology
Disseminated zoster (>3 dermatomes or >20 lesions outside primary dermatome) → immunocompromised — antiviral IV urgently
Bacterial superinfection of vesicles (purulent, spreading cellulitis)
ddx: Zosteriform HSV (HSV-1 in dermatomal pattern — requires PCR to distinguish)

first_line.name: Oral Antiviral (within 72h of rash onset)
first_line.drugs: Valacyclovir (Valtrex) 1000 mg TID × 7 days | Famciclovir 500 mg TID × 7 days | Acyclovir 800 mg 5× daily × 7 days
first_line.notes: Most effective when started within 72h of rash onset (can still treat if new vesicles forming after 72h). Reduces severity and duration of acute pain; may reduce acute pain severity — benefit for PHN prevention is uncertain per Cochrane review. Valacyclovir preferred — TID dosing (vs acyclovir's 5×/day) improves adherence; equivalent efficacy. Better bioavailability as a valacyclovir prodrug. Renal dose adjustment: Valacyclovir: CrCl 30–49 mL/min → 1000 mg BID × 7 days; CrCl 10–29 → 1000 mg OD × 7 days; CrCl <10 → 500 mg OD × 7 days.
first_line.preferred_when: All patients ≥50, all immunocompromised, all with ophthalmic involvement — start within 72h

first_line.name: Pain Management
first_line.drugs: Gabapentin: start 100–300 mg at bedtime, titrate to 100–300 mg TID as tolerated (especially in elderly — start low) | Pregabalin 75 mg BID (titrate to 150 mg BID) as alternative | Amitriptyline 10 mg at bedtime (max 25–50 mg) — use with caution in elderly | NSAIDs / acetaminophen for acute pain
first_line.notes: Postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) is the most important complication — burning, shooting pain persisting months after rash resolves (defined as pain >90 days post-rash onset). Gabapentin or amitriptyline started acutely may reduce PHN risk. Amitriptyline: use with caution in elderly (anticholinergic effects, fall risk, QTc prolongation) — start 10 mg at bedtime; max 25–50 mg. Capsaicin 8% patch: for established PHN — specialist-applied.

non_pharm: Cool compresses (calamine lotion) to vesicles — reduces itch and pain
Keep rash clean and dry; loose clothing
Contagious: avoid contact with non-immune (especially pregnant women and newborns) until vesicles fully crusted
Lidocaine 5% patch: for established postherpetic neuralgia (PHN — pain >90 days post-rash onset)

Shingrix vaccine (recombinant zoster vaccine, RZV): NACI (2022) recommends for: (1) adults ≥50 years, (2) immunocompromised adults ≥18 years. 2 doses 2–6 months apart — 97% effective. Ontario UIIP publicly funds Shingrix for ages 65–70. Wait ≥6–12 months after acute zoster episode before vaccinating.
refer_when: Ophthalmic involvement (V1 distribution, tip of nose — Hutchinson sign) → urgent same-day ophthalmology (corneal scarring risk)
Ear/otic involvement (Ramsay Hunt syndrome: ear pain + vesicles in ear canal + facial palsy) → urgent ENT/neurology
Disseminated zoster (>3 dermatomes or bilateral) → immunocompromised — IV antiviral urgently
Rash >72h old in high-risk patients (can still benefit if new vesicles forming)

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Herpes Zoster (Shingles). OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
NACI (National Advisory Committee on Immunization). Recommendations on the Use of Herpes Zoster Vaccines. Canada Communicable Disease Report. 2018;44(11):274–293 (updated 2023).
Dworkin RH, et al. Recommendations for the Management of Herpes Zoster. Clin Infect Dis. 2007;44(Suppl 1):S1–S26. (IDSA, endorsed by AAD/AAO)
NICE. Shingles (herpes zoster): Clinical Knowledge Summary (CKS). National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2023).
